key_missing_perspectives: Sabah/Sarawak indigenous groups with internal variation masked by averages
Stateless/undocumented (no MyKad, cannot access e-wallets or subsidies)
Malay rural youth specific risk profile
Women domestic violence survivors (cashless removes emergency escape resource)
key_alternative_framings: Bureaucratic inertia + institutional fragmentation, not political cynicism — BNM/MOF/MCMC/Home Ministry each own pieces, no lead agency
Real divide is income, not payment instrument — credit cards concentrated in T20/M40; B40 uses cash/e-wallets/basic accounts
Path dependence not incompetence — Malaysia chose speed over safety as deliberate policy
international_parallels_added: Indonesia QRIS: central dispute switch, tiered IDR 2B capital requirement
Philippines GCash: Send Money Protect insurance as license condition
UK FSCS: £85,000 coverage for e-money in segregated accounts, tested in Wirecard insolvency
Australia ePayments Code: strict liability on providers unless user negligence proven
key_counter_arguments: To card 2: Deposit insurance premiums would be passed to users; B40 typically holds RM50-100 average — insurance cost could exceed balance
To card 3: RM2.77B includes all online fraud, not e-wallet specific. Method breakdown 'not centrally collected' per Home Ministry

To card 5: Re Kayford (1975) English common law principles make trust beneficial ownership more established than claimed. Real risk is operator misappropriation, which deposit insurance doesn't prevent either
key_missing_facts: BNM 2024: total e-wallet float RM2.1B at Dec 2024, 78% in top 3 operators. 63% of accounts have balances below RM50 (importance 0.7)
FMOS 2025: 1,843 e-wallet complaints, only 22% resolved in consumer favour, avg 89 days (target 45) (importance 0.8)
Lim v Touch 'n Go (2023, unreported) — High Court dismissed unauthorised transaction claim citing gross negligence (importance 0.9)
Consumer Credit Act 2025 Section 2(3) excludes e-wallet credit features — regulatory gap (importance 0.6)
gender_gaps: Women's digital literacy 12% lower than men's in rural Malaysia (DOSM 2025)
WAO 2025: 34% of survivors cite financial control via digital payments as barrier to leaving
economic_gaps: Tiered protection needed — T20 RM400 avg vs B40 small balances representing larger share of liquid wealth
RM2B subsidies included hidden corporate welfare to operators via merchant fees (RM16-32M estimated)
geographic_gaps: Sabah interior districts (Nabawan, Pitas, Kinabatangan) have <10% e-wallet penetration due to 4G gaps
Kelantan/Terengganu adoption <20% — religious/cultural factors, not just infrastructure
overall_assessment: Strong on legal, fraud, policy timing. Misses Orang Asli, stateless, domestic violence survivors, rural Malay youth. Lacks counter-arguments on cost of deposit insurance and trust protection adequacy. Missing FMOS complaint data, Lim case, and BNM float distribution would significantly reshape the legal uncertainty claim.
